advantages: Often state-of-the-art accuracy on tabular data with minimal feature engineering beyond raw numeric and categorical columns.
Sequential residual fitting automatically discovers non-linearities and feature interactions through successive shallow trees.
Shrinkage (learning rate) and shallow trees provide built-in regularization compared with a single deep tree.
Supports many loss functions via the same gradient-boosting template (regression, classification, robust losses).
disadvantages: Training is sequential—harder to parallelize across trees than random forests, so large n_estimators can be slow on CPU-only setups.
Sensitive to hyperparameters (n_estimators, learning_rate, max_depth); poor tuning leads to overfitting or underfitting.
Less natural for unstructured inputs (raw images, long text) without heavy feature extraction compared with deep learning.
Full ensemble inference requires summing many trees; latency and model size grow with n_estimators unless distilled or pruned.
